text: The surgeon performs a left-sided resection, beginning with the ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery and vein. Dissection starts at the sacral promontory with an incision in the parietal peritoneum. The inferior mesenteric artery is dissected using sharp and blunt techniques, secured with hemlock clips, and divided, opening the medial to lateral plane. Dissection continues between the mesocolon and Girotta's fascia, reaching the lower border of the pancreas where a swab is placed for flexure mobilization. The inferior mesenteric vein is divided between clips, completing the medial to lateral dissection. The remaining thin layer of lateral colon attachments is released using hook endothermy.

The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg for splenic flexure mobilization, beginning with gastrocolic omental dissection and release of the transverse colon using a harmonic scalpel. The swab placed above the pancreas is visible. The renocolic ligament is divided to completely mobilize the colon laterally. The patient is then repositioned in Trendelenburg with left tilt for iliocolic vascular dissection, identifying the duodenum as a landmark for safe division of the vascular pedicle. Alukolic vessels are secured with hemlock clips and divided, with a swab placed above the duodenum. Right colon mobilization follows, using hook diathermy with adequate traction and counter traction. The hepatic flexure is mobilized by dividing the gastrocolic omentum, with the swab above the duodenum visible.

The surgeon manages the middle colic vessels by dissecting from both the right and left sides, with assistance lifting the mesocolon. The right and left branches of the mesocolic vessels are secured with hemolog clips and divided, resulting in the complete mobilization of the colon.

The surgeon performs a proctectomy in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, beginning dissection posteriorly behind the superior rectal artery using diathermy for precise separation in front of the presacral fascia while preserving hypogastric nerves. Anterior dissection is marked at the peritoneal reflection level, with a pledget providing counter traction. Dissection proceeds circumferentially down to the pelvic floor, completely mobilizing the rectum by releasing the left lateral sidewall. Anterior dissection continues within the Denonvilliers fascia to protect the nerves.

The surgeon uses proline sutures to hitch up the peritoneal reflection, enhancing access and view. Dissection proceeds behind the seminal vesicles anteriorly with a harmonic scalpel, reaching the pelvic floor. The rectal tube is dissected, and a rectal examination is performed to assess the level of rectal transaction. The rectum is divided anterior-posteriorly using a roticulating endo-GIA stapler, with two firings of a forty-five millimeter stapler.

The surgeon extracts the specimen through the umbilicus and constructs an ileal J pouch using a linear cutter stapler, requiring two to three firings to create a fifteen-centimeter pouch. A CDH-29 circular stapler is used for the ileoanal anastomosis, performed under direct vision. The small bowel is oriented correctly by following the mesentery line from the duodenal-jejunal flexure to the ileoanal anastomosis. An intraoperative sigmoidoscopy is conducted to check the anastomosis.
